title: Confirmation: The Gift of Fortitude
html:
The Big Question

What would you do, at 14 years old, if they told you: “Deny Christ or we'll kill you”?

On the night of February 10, 1928, in the Mexican town of Sahuayo, Michoacán, a boy of 14 named José Sánchez del Río was dragged from prison by federal soldiers. Mexico was burning in the Cristero War, a conflict in which thousands of Catholics rose up to defend their faith against a government that had banned all public worship.

José was an ordinary kid — he liked to play, run, and laugh — but he had received Confirmation. He had been anointed with chrism as a soldier of Christ. And when the moment came, that skinny, dark-haired boy proved that the Holy Spirit doesn't measure age.

The soldiers cut the soles of his feet and forced him to walk barefoot toward the cemetery. Along the way, every time he stumbled and fell, they shouted at him: “Renounce your faith!” And José, with shredded feet and blood marking the ground, answered again and again: “¡Viva Cristo Rey! Long live Christ the King!”

When they reached the cemetery, they asked him to deny Christ one last time. He replied: “I've never done anything wrong. I'm going to Heaven, and I'll see you there.” The commander, furious, shot him while he kept shouting: “Long live Christ the King!”

St. José Sánchez del Río — canonized by Pope Francis in 2016 — is living proof of what Confirmation can do. He wasn't Superman. He was a regular teenager, anointed by the Holy Spirit, who knew where his strength came from.

Five centuries earlier, another teenager — St. Joan of Arc, a 17-year-old French peasant girl — had heard heavenly voices telling her: “Daughter of God, go, save France.” And she, a girl who couldn't read or ride a horse, put on armor, led armies, liberated cities… and when she was captured and burned alive, her last words were not of hatred but of love: “Jesus, Jesus, Jesus!”

The Catechism says that Confirmation gives us “a special strength of the Holy Spirit to spread and defend the faith” (CCC 1285). José and Joan weren't superheroes. They were confirmed young people. What they did wasn't the work of their personality — it was the work of the Holy Spirit dwelling within them since their Confirmation.

This lesson isn't about learning what Confirmation is. It's about understanding who comes to dwell in you on that day… and what would happen if you truly let him act.

Activity

Imagine you have a friend who says: “I got confirmed, so I'm done. I don't have to go to church anymore.” What would you say to them? Is Confirmation a “graduation” or a “commissioning”? Think of a confirmed friend or family member who has left the Church, and another who remains faithful. What's the difference between them? Draw a shield (like a knight's) and write inside the “powers” you think Confirmation would give you to defend your faith.

1. What Is Confirmation? Your Personal Pentecost

The Catechism defines Confirmation with a sentence that is a life program: “The sacrament of Confirmation perfects baptismal grace; it is the sacrament which gives the Holy Spirit in order to root us more deeply in the divine filiation, incorporate us more firmly into Christ, strengthen our bond with the Church, associate us more closely with her mission, and help us bear witness to the Christian faith in words accompanied by deeds” (CCC 1285, quoting Paul VI's Divinae consortium naturae).

But let's put it more simply: Confirmation is your personal Pentecost.

At Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4), the Holy Spirit descended on the apostles like a driving wind, in the form of tongues of fire. Those fishermen who had been hiding in fear went out into the streets to preach with such courage that they converted three thousand people in a single day. Peter, who days earlier had denied Jesus when questioned by a servant girl, now faced the crowds of Jerusalem. That is the action of the Holy Spirit: he transforms cowards into witnesses.

In Confirmation, the same Holy Spirit descends upon you. Not in the form of visible fire — that was Pentecost, which is unique — but with the same real power. Confirmation is the sacrament of the Holy Spirit par excellence (CCC 1288). In Baptism you received new life; in Confirmation you receive the spiritual maturity to live and defend that life (CCC 1289).

Context

In the early Church, the three sacraments of Christian initiation (Baptism, Confirmation, and Eucharist) were celebrated together, especially at the Easter Vigil. The bishop would baptize, immediately confirm, and then the new Christian would receive their first Communion. This order remains visible and in force in the Eastern Catholic and Orthodox Churches (CCC 1290).

In the West (the Latin Church, which is ours), the practice separated for pastoral reasons: as the number of baptisms grew, there were not enough bishops to attend every baptism, and Confirmation was reserved for a later moment when the bishop could visit the community. But theologically, Confirmation and Baptism are two sides of the same coin: Baptism gives you the life; Confirmation gives you the strength to live that life.

St. Cyprian of Carthage (3rd century) already used the image of the “double sacrament”: “It is necessary that he who has been baptized should also be anointed, so that, having received the chrism, that is the anointing, he may be the anointed of God and have in himself the grace of Christ.”

2. Biblical Foundation: The Laying on of Hands from the Apostles

Confirmation is not a medieval invention. It is in the most ancient pages of the New Testament, in the Acts of the Apostles. The apostles laid hands on the newly baptized so that they might receive the Holy Spirit, in a rite distinct from Baptism (CCC 1286-1288).

The foundational text is that of Samaria:

“Now when the apostles at Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent to them Peter and John, who came down and prayed for them that they might receive the Holy Spirit; for he had not yet fallen on any of them, but they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. Then they laid their hands on them and they received the Holy Spirit.” Acts 8:14-17

Notice this well: these people had already been baptized, but they had not yet received the fullness of the Holy Spirit. A subsequent rite is needed: the laying on of hands by the apostles (Peter and John, the leaders of the Church). Today, bishops are the successors of the apostles (CCC 1313). The Confirmation we celebrate today is exactly the same rite that those first Christians of Samaria experienced.

St. Paul does the same in Ephesus:

“And when Paul had laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came on them, and they began speaking in tongues and prophesying.” Acts 19:6

The rite developed: to the laying on of hands was added the anointing with sacred chrism (perfumed oil consecrated by the bishop), to express more clearly the gift of the Holy Spirit. The word “Christian” comes from the Greek christianos, “anointed.” Through Baptism we are anointed; through Confirmation, our anointing is perfected (CCC 1291).

Connection

Read Acts 2:1-4 (Pentecost) and then Isaiah 11:2 (the seven gifts of the Spirit). Then ask yourself: if the same Spirit who descended on the apostles descends on you at Confirmation… what should change in the way you live?

St. John Paul II, in his last book “Rise, Let Us Be on Our Way,” recounted that on the day of his Confirmation (at age 12, in Wadowice, Poland), the bishop gave him a light tap on the cheek (an ancient gesture in the rite that symbolized readiness for combat), and young Karol felt he had been enlisted in something great. Decades later, as Pope, he survived an assassination attempt and forgave his attacker. That is Confirmation at work.

3. The Seven Gifts of the Holy Spirit: The Christian's Equipment

On the day of your Confirmation, the Holy Spirit equips you with seven gifts. They're not “magic powers” or things to show off. They are supernatural habits that dispose you to follow the promptings of the Spirit. Isaiah enumerated them seven centuries before Christ, describing the future Messiah:

“There shall come forth a shoot from the stump of Jesse, and a branch shall grow out of his roots. And the Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon him, the spirit of wisdom and understanding, the spirit of counsel and might, the spirit of knowledge and the fear of the Lord.” Isaiah 11:1-2

The Christian tradition has broken down these seven gifts as follows (CCC 1831), and it's worth knowing them one by one, because they are the supernatural survival kit of the confirmed Christian:

  • Wisdom: not “being smart,” but savoring God, delighting in divine things. The gift of wisdom makes you see the world from God's perspective: everything good comes from him, every suffering can be united to the Cross, every life has an eternal purpose. It is the gift that lets you say with St. Paul: “I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord” (Phil 3:8).
  • Understanding: penetrating the truths of faith beyond the surface. A person with the gift of understanding doesn't settle for “because they told me so”; they grasp why the Church teaches what she teaches. They see connections. They understand that the Mass isn't just any ritual, but Calvary made present; that the Pope isn't a CEO, but the Vicar of Christ.
  • Counsel: illuminates the conscience in concrete decisions. It's not “asking everyone's opinion” but tuning in to the voice of the Spirit telling you: “this way, not that way.” St. John Bosco said that the guardian angel speaks through the gift of counsel. When a young person weighs a career that will make them rich against one that will make them a saint, the gift of counsel brings clarity.
  • Fortitude: the gift most identified with Confirmation. It's not the absence of fear; it's acting in spite of fear. It's the gift of Peter facing the Sanhedrin. It's the gift of José Sánchez del Río walking on his flayed feet. It's the gift of the mother who prays the Rosary with her family even when everyone mocks her. It's the gift that lets you go to Confession even when you're embarrassed.
  • Knowledge: not biology or physics. It's the ability to read the created world as the footprint of God. The gift of knowledge makes you see that all creatures are limited and point beyond themselves. It protects you from idolizing good things: money, beauty, health, relationships… because you understand that none of them is God, and using them as if they were is the fastest path to frustration.
  • Piety: the gift that makes you feel like a child of God, not an orphan. It gives you filial trust. It's not sentimentality, but the deep certainty that God loves you personally and that you can talk to him like a child talking to their dad. Piety heals the twisted image of a stern God watching to punish you. It shows you the Father of the prodigal son waiting with open arms.
  • Fear of the Lord: the most misunderstood. It's not servile fear, like the slave who fears the whip. It's filial fear: the fear of offending the one you love, of disappointing your Father. Like the son who doesn't want to sadden his mother — not out of fear of punishment, but out of love. Fear of the Lord is the delicacy of the soul that would rather die than commit mortal sin. It is, paradoxically, the summit of freedom.

These gifts do not expire. They are not just for Confirmation day; they remain in the soul as permanent capacities. But they need to be activated through prayer, the sacraments, and good works. An unlived Confirmation is like a state-of-the-art phone with a dead battery: you have all the capabilities (the sacramental character), but you don't use them.

4. The Rite of Confirmation: Matter, Form, and Minister

The matter of the sacrament is sacred chrism, olive oil perfumed with balsam and consecrated by the bishop at the Chrism Mass on Holy Thursday (CCC 1297, 1300). The oil symbolizes strength and flexibility; the perfume, the spread of the good news. Chrism is the “apt matter” for anointing, and is also used in Baptism and priestly Ordination — it is the seal of Christian consecration.

The form (the necessary words) is: “Be sealed with the Gift of the Holy Spirit,” pronounced while the bishop traces the sign of the cross on your forehead with chrism (CCC 1300).

But the full rite is richer. The Catechism describes these stages (CCC 1298-1301):

  1. Renewal of baptismal promises: the confirmand stands up and renews the faith. The bishop asks: “Do you renounce Satan?” “I do!” “Do you believe in God the Father almighty…?” It is the adult profession of faith. At Baptism, others answered for you; now you answer for yourself.
  2. Laying on of hands: the bishop extends his hands over all the confirmands and prays: “Almighty God, Father of our Lord Jesus Christ… send your Holy Spirit, the Paraclete, upon these your children….” This gesture is exactly that of the Apostles in Samaria (Acts 8:17).
  3. Anointing with chrism: the bishop (or delegated priest) anoints the forehead of each confirmand, tracing the sign of the cross, while saying: “Be sealed with the Gift of the Holy Spirit.” The confirmand answers: “Amen.”
  4. The kiss of peace: the bishop greets the newly confirmed: “Peace be with you.” It is the embrace of welcome into the adult Church, the ecclesial communion sealed with the bishop, the visible sign of unity.

The ordinary minister of Confirmation is the bishop (CCC 1312-1313). Why? For two reasons: first, because he is the successor of the apostles (it was they who laid hands in Samaria and Ephesus). Second, because Confirmation unites the confirmand to the universal Church, and the bishop is the visible sign of that unity. As St. Jerome said: “Where the bishop is, there is the Church.”

However, a priest can confirm by delegation from the bishop (for example, in far-flung parishes or at the Easter Vigil), and can always confirm in danger of death (CCC 1314). In the Eastern Churches, the priest confirms immediately after Baptism as a general norm, using chrism blessed by the bishop. The Latin Church permits both practices but maintains the bishop as the ordinary minister.

Going Deeper

Did you know that in the early centuries, at the end of Confirmation, the bishop would give the confirmand a light tap on the cheek? It was a gesture that came from the medieval ceremony of knighting, which the Church adapted as a symbol that the confirmed Christian is ready for spiritual combat. Confirmation enlists you in the militia Christi, the military service of Christ, and the enemy is not flesh and blood (Eph 6:12).

The term miles Christi (soldier of Christ) appears already in Tertullian (3rd century) and was developed by St. Augustine and St. Thomas. It does not mean violence, but total surrender. Just as the soldier has no other occupation than serving, the confirmed Christian is called to make their entire life a service to God and to others. “No soldier on service entangles himself in the affairs of this life, that he may please him who enlisted him as a soldier” (2 Tim 2:4).

St. Thomas Aquinas (Summa Theologiae III, q.72, a.2) explains that Confirmation gives the perfect age of the spiritual life. Just as in natural life a child is not equipped to fight until reaching a certain maturity, in the supernatural life Confirmation enables you for spiritual combat. That is why it is received at the age of reason or at the beginning of adolescence: at the moment when you begin making personal decisions that will mark your whole life.

5. The Effects: Five Concrete Transformations

The Catechism lists the effects of Confirmation (CCC 1302-1305) systematically. But merely listing them isn't enough. We need to understand them vitally:

  • 1. It unites us more deeply to Christ: the Holy Spirit configures us with Christ the Head. You are no longer a “sympathizer” of Jesus or a “customer” of his services. You are “another Christ” (alter Christus) and you are called to act in his name.
  • 2. It increases in us the gifts of the Holy Spirit: the seven gifts we saw earlier are not received for the first time in Confirmation (they were already in Baptism in seed form), but here they are poured out with new and permanent power. It's like going from a dripping tap to a fire hose.
  • 3. It imprints an indelible character on the soul: like Baptism, Confirmation marks the soul forever. It is a seal that even the gravest sin cannot erase. It means that, once confirmed, you belong to Christ in an irreversible way. You may drift away, but you will always carry that mark. That is why Confirmation cannot be repeated (CCC 1304-1305).
  • 4. It perfects our union with the Church: the confirmed person is an adult member of the community. No longer just a recipient but also an agent. You have the responsibility to build up the Church, to evangelize, to serve. You can't say “let others do it.”
  • 5. It gives us strength to be witnesses of Christ: this is the most specific effect. The confirmed person is a witness (in Greek, martys, from which we get “martyr”). Some will be called to the martyrdom of blood (like José Sánchez del Río). All of us are called to the martyrdom of daily life: defending the faith in workplace conversations, not staying silent when people mock the Church, living chastity in a world that despises it, being honest when everyone cheats.

Key Idea: Confirmation is not the “graduation” from catechesis — it is the ordination of the laity for mission. As St. John Paul II wrote in Christifideles laici: the confirmed are “the vanguard of the Church in the world.”

6. At What Age? The Pastoral Debate and the Deeper Meaning

If Confirmation is “Christian maturity,” an inevitable question arises: at what age should it be received? The Latin Church has not fixed a universal age; she leaves each episcopal conference to decide according to circumstances (CCC 1307). In practice, there are two positions:

Some hold that it should be administered around age 7 (together with First Communion), restoring the ancient order of the sacraments of initiation: Baptism–Confirmation–Eucharist. They argue that the gifts of the Holy Spirit are necessary from childhood, especially in a world hostile to faith. This is the practice of the Eastern Churches and some Western dioceses.

Others (the majority in Latin America and many European dioceses) prefer adolescence (12–17 years). The argument is that Confirmation requires a mature personal decision, something a 7-year-old child cannot make. Moreover, adolescence is the age of the most intense spiritual combat: it is when the young person defines their identity, faces peer pressure, and decides what kind of person they want to be. They need the Holy Spirit at that precise moment.

The problem is NOT the age. The real problem is impoverished sacramental pastoral practice: when Confirmation is reduced to a bureaucratic formality (“come to three talks, pay the stipend, and you're done”), the age doesn't matter, because grace finds no prepared ground. The solution is not to change the age but to recover a serious preparation that includes: solid doctrinal formation, sacramental life (frequent Confession and Communion), personal accompaniment, and experience of service to the poor. When that happens, even a child can receive Confirmation fruitfully; and without it, not even an adult is prepared.

The Catechism asks that the confirmand have attained the use of reason, profess the faith, be in the state of grace, and have the intention of receiving the sacrament (CCC 1307-1308). Nothing more. But it also asks that the preparation “have as its aim to lead the Christian toward a more intimate union with Christ, a deeper familiarity with the Holy Spirit… and a more profound insertion into the life of the Church” (CCC 1309). If the preparation fails to achieve this, the sacrament is received but doesn't bear fruit. That is the true crisis.

7. Confirmation in Salvation History: The Holy Spirit as the Protagonist

Confirmation is not an isolated rite. It is a link in a chain that runs through the whole history of salvation. From the beginning, the Spirit of God has been active:

  • In Creation: “The Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters” (Gen 1:2).
  • In the Prophets: he anointed kings and prophets for mission (1 Sam 16:13). Isaiah foretold the seven gifts of the Messiah (Is 11:2). Ezekiel saw the valley of dry bones come to life through the breath of the Spirit (Ez 37).
  • In the Incarnation: “The Holy Spirit will come upon you” (Lk 1:35). Jesus was conceived by the Holy Spirit and anointed by him at his Baptism (Lk 3:22). The word “Christ” means “the Anointed One.” Jesus is full of the Holy Spirit.
  • At Pentecost: the Holy Spirit descends upon Mary and the apostles and the Church is born (Acts 2).
  • In the sacraments: the Holy Spirit acts in each sacrament, but in Confirmation he gives himself in a special way as the Gift (CCC 1303).
  • In the life of the Christian: the Holy Spirit dwells in you from Baptism (1 Cor 3:16: “Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?”). In Confirmation, this indwelling is intensified. As St. Paul says: “God's love has been poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit who has been given to us” (Rom 5:5).

St. Paul reminds us that we were sealed by the Spirit:

“In him you also, when you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation, and believed in him, were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit.” Ephesians 1:13

And in 1 Corinthians 12 he details how the same Spirit distributes various gifts for the good of the whole Church (1 Cor 12:4-11). Confirmation inserts you into that dynamic: you no longer live for yourself, but for the Body of Christ.

For Reflection

1. If you have received Confirmation, what was your preparation like? Did it really prepare you to live an adult faith, or was it more of a formality you fulfilled?

Be honest. Many young people see Confirmation as a social or family requirement. The grace was given (and the sacramental character is in your soul), but if the ground was not prepared, the seed of the Spirit lies dormant. What could you do today to “reactivate” that grace?

2. Of the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit, which one do you most urgently need at this moment in your life?

Wisdom to not be deceived by the world? Fortitude to overcome a sin that dominates you? Counsel to make an important decision? Piety to rediscover prayer? Identify it and ask for it. The Holy Spirit gives his gifts without measure to those who ask with humility.

3. Confirmation makes us soldiers of Christ (miles Christi), but the enemy is not human. What spiritual battles are you fighting right now?

Don't be ashamed. We all fight battles: spiritual laziness, discouragement, impurity, pride, anger, envy… St. Paul says: “We do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the principalities, against the powers, against the world rulers of this present darkness” (Eph 6:12). Are you fighting with your own weapons or with the armor the Spirit gives you?

4. Think about St. José Sánchez del Río: he was 14 when they killed him. His torturers said they heard heavenly music. What do you think when you contrast your faith with his?

It's not about comparing martyrdoms. It's about asking yourself: if he, at 14 years old with a handful of catechism classes, could give his life for Christ… what excuse do I have for not being a witness in small things: an uncomfortable conversation, an honest decision, a daily prayer?

5. If the Holy Spirit truly dwells in you and has anointed you for mission, what would change in your life if you started today to live as a truly confirmed Christian?

Imagine that, starting tomorrow, you act in every situation asking yourself: “What would a Christian anointed by the Holy Spirit do here?” How would your relationship with your family change, your way of studying or working, your way of having fun?

Cultural Wealth

“For Greater Glory” (Cristiada, 2012) — Directed by Dean Wright and starring Andy García, Eva Longoria, and Peter O'Toole, this film tells the story of the Cristero War (1926–1929) and the martyrdom of the young José Sánchez del Río. The scene of his martyrdom is one of the most powerful in recent religious cinema. Available on Netflix and YouTube Movies. Recommended: watch before or after this lesson, especially for teenagers and young adults.
St. Joan of Arc in Art and Film — The figure of Joan of Arc has inspired masterpieces: the silent film The Passion of Joan of Arc (Carl Theodor Dreyer, 1928) is considered one of the best films in the history of cinema; Luc Besson's The Messenger: The Story of Joan of Arc (1999) offers a more modern view. In literature, Mark Twain wrote Personal Recollections of Joan of Arc (1896), which he considered his greatest book — a passionate defense of the saint.
“The Song of Bernadette” (1943) — Film about St. Bernadette of Lourdes. Although it focuses on the apparitions at Lourdes, Jennifer Jones won the Oscar portraying a teenage peasant girl who couldn't read but who faced Church and civil authorities with the strength of the Spirit. A gem about how God chooses the lowly to confound the wise. Available on classic streaming platforms.
“The Inspiration of Saint Matthew” by Caravaggio (1602) — Caravaggio painted this masterpiece in the Contarelli Chapel of San Luigi dei Francesi (Rome). It shows an angel dictating to St. Matthew what he must write. The light illuminating Matthew is not natural; it is the light of the Holy Spirit inspiring. Matthew's face expresses effort and attention: he is not writing as an automaton; he is cooperating. This is how the Spirit of the Confirmed acts: he does not annihilate your personality, he elevates it. Viewable in high resolution on Wikimedia Commons.
Taizé Music — The ecumenical Community of Taizé (France) has created a unique musical style: short, repetitive, meditative chants, such as the famous Veni Sancte Spiritus (“Come, Holy Spirit”). Thousands of young people make pilgrimage to Taizé every year and discover that the Holy Spirit is not an abstract idea but a presence experienced in silence and sung prayer. Spotify has official Taizé playlists. For Confirmation day, singing Veni Sancte Spiritus (Taizé) or the hymn Veni Creator Spiritus (9th-century Gregorian chant) is most fitting.
Hakuna Group Music — A phenomenon of contemporary Catholic music born in Spain and spread throughout Latin America and the United States. Songs like “Huracán,” “Solo Tú,” or “Mi Pobre Loco” speak of a personal relationship with Christ, with lyrics expressing interior fire, desire for holiness, abandonment in God. For young people seeking a current musical language that is unapologetically Catholic, Hakuna is ideal. Their concerts fill stadiums. Available on Spotify, YouTube, and hakunagroupmusic.com.
The YouCat — The Youth Catechism (YouCat, questions 200–206) offers a brief and powerful explanation of Confirmation. Available free in the YouCat app and at youcat.org. For this lesson, reading questions 200–203 provides a perfect complement. The YouCat also has the DOCAT (social doctrine) that confirmed young people can use to learn about the social commitment that flows from Confirmation.
Ascension Presents (Fr. Mike Schmitz) — Search for “Confirmation” on this popular YouTube channel. Fr. Mike, who also hosts the Bible in a Year and Catechism in a Year podcasts, explains Confirmation with clarity and energy. Excellent supplementary resource.
Real + True (OSV) — A multimedia Catechism project at osvnews.com/real-true. Their episode on CCC 1285–1321 covers Confirmation in accessible, modern language. Also, the Word on Fire series by Bishop Robert Barron has excellent short videos on the Holy Spirit and the sacraments.
“Into Great Silence” (2005) — A documentary film by Philip Gröning about the Carthusian monks of the Grande Chartreuse monastery in France. Nearly three hours long, with almost no dialogue, it is a visual meditation on contemplative silence. Watching even 15 minutes shows what it means to live entirely attentive to the Holy Spirit. An antidote to the noise of modern life — and a powerful introduction to the contemplative dimension that Confirmation awakens.
“Karol: A Man Who Became Pope” — Miniseries about St. John Paul II. The scene of his Confirmation in Wadowice is brief but revealing: the bishop gives him a tap on the cheek, and young Karol (Piotr Adamczyk) leaves the church different. Years later, in occupied Poland, that same Spirit will sustain him in the underground seminary.
